items.correct: A fatal error or flaw in the tragic hero that leads to their downfall — sometimes a character defect, sometimes a mistake in judgement.
items.misconception: Hamartia is the tragic hero's evil personality.
items.random_wrong: Hamartia is the moment the tragic hero dies.
items.why: Tragic heroes are not villains. Hamartia is often a noble quality taken too far (Othello's love, Macbeth's ambition) or a misjudgement — not simple wickedness.

items.correct: Experimentation with form (fragmentation, stream of consciousness), focus on subjective interior experience, scepticism toward traditional narrative and absolute truth.
items.misconception: Modernism just means literature written in modern times.
items.random_wrong: Modernism is literature about modern technology like phones.
items.why: Modernism is a specific early 20th-century movement (Joyce, Woolf, Eliot), not a synonym for contemporary. Students use it as a vague chronological term, missing its formal experimentation.

items.q: Why must students distinguish the narrator from the author?
items.correct: The narrator is a constructed voice within the text; the author is the real person who created it. Conflating them misreads irony, unreliable narration and persona.
items.misconception: The narrator's views are always the author's views.
items.random_wrong: The narrator and author are different words for the same thing.
items.why: Students attribute everything a first-person narrator says to the author. This collapses irony — a novel like 'Lolita' cannot work if you take Humbert as Nabokov.

items.q: Which feminist critic's 1982 study highlighted the sexualisation of Tess by Hardy's own narrator?
items.why: Correct answer: Penny Boumelha. “Elaine Showalter” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which Marxist critic reads Tess as a victim of rural capitalist disintegration?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Raymond Williams. “Terry Eagleton” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In which year did Penny Boumelha publish 'Thomas Hardy and Women'?
items.why: Exact answer: 1982. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
